entries.text: Today in this video, you will see Korean
entertainment stars speaking English,
Spanish, and even French with real video
proof showing who actually masters other
languages and who just memorizes lines.
When Anio speaks English, there is no
effort, no forced accent, and certainly
no memorized phrases. What you hear is
real fluency. Born in South Korea, he
moved to Toronto, Canada as a child,
where he spent over a decade of his
life. It was there that he studied, grew
up, and formed his cultural identity. In
fact, during this period, An Hyosop used
the English name Paul, something common
among young people who truly live
abroad, not just study the language.
This explains why his English sounds so
natural, so smooth, and so confident. He
isn't translating thoughts from Korean.
He is simply speaking.
>> Hi, El Singapore. This is Anuzo, and I'm
[music] here to spill some.
>> His fluency opened real doors in his
international career, such as his voice
acting work in global Netflix
productions, including animated projects
aimed at Western audiences. An hio isn't
just a Korean actor who speaks English.
He is a global actor who switches
between languages without losing his
naturalness. If there is anyone whose
energy shifts completely when changing
languages, it is Chuik. Many people know
the intense, introspective and emotional
side of his characters in Kdramas and
Korean cinema. But when he starts
speaking English, he is a different
person.
>> Wiki and I play it on an ordinary
college student.
>> Chi Wik moved to Vancouver, Canada at
the age of 10. It was there that he grew
up, studied, and built his personality.
He even uses the English name Edward.
The result is absolutely natural English
with that casual and laid-back tone
typical of someone who really lived the
culture. We have already seen this
fluency in productions like Parasite,
Oakia, and more recently in
international interviews that went viral
precisely because the contrast is
fascinating. He doesn't just express
himself in English. He conveys humor,
irony, and charisma, things that only
appear when someone completely masters a
language. When Seo Yay Ji starts
speaking Spanish, it becomes immediately
clear that this is not part of a
character, let alone a memorized script.
What we see there is someone who
actually lived the language, and that
changes everything. Before becoming one
of the most talked about actresses in
Kdramas, Seoa Gi spent an important
period of her life living in Spain. And
when someone lives in another country,
the language stops being just a study
subject. It becomes part of their
thinking, emotional reaction, and way of
expressing themselves. That is exactly
why her Spanish sounds so natural. There
are no long pauses, no visible effort to
construct sentences, [music] and most
importantly, there is no artificial
intonation that usually appears when
actors merely memorize lines in another
tongue. In some of her dramas, Seo Ya Ji
even speaks Spanish surprisingly fluidly
with complete sentences, good
pronunciation, and a confidence that
catches the attention even of native
speakers. This is not common even among
international actors. The most
impressive part is that she never turned
this into a marketing trick. She doesn't
force situations to show she speaks
Spanish. She simply speaks when the
context calls for it. And it is exactly
this naturalenness that confirms the
real level of her fluency. Sio doesn't
speak Spanish to impress. She speaks it
because she knows it.
>> When Park Bogum speaks Spanish, most
people's reaction is always the same.
Surprise. This is because no one expects
one of the most classic and delicate
faces of Kdramas to simply start
communicating in another language with
such calm and clarity. But this skill
didn't come out of nowhere. In the drama
Encounter, Park Bogum plays a character
who travels to Cuba. And instead of
opting for generic or simplified
phrases, he really made an effort to
learn functional Spanish so that his
interactions would sound natural within
the story.
>> Being in LA for the first time.
>> Yeah, it's my very first time to MC in
here. So, I'm so happy to be here. And
>> the result is visible, even if it isn't
native level Spanish. The pronunciation
is clear, the intonation is correct, and
above all, there is real intention in
his speech. He doesn't seem to be just
repeating sounds. He is communicating.
The most interesting thing is that Park
Bogum has always been known for his
extreme dedication to everything he
does. Whether learning musical
instruments, perfecting [music] his
acting, or studying languages, he treats
every detail with respect and
seriousness. [music]
With Spanish, it is no different. Every
word is spoken with care. Every sentence
is delivered with attention. And [music]
this creates an immediate connection
with the Latin audience. Perhaps Park
Bog Gum isn't the most fluent Korean
actor in Spanish on this list, but he is
undoubtedly one of the most respectful
toward the language, and that makes all
the difference. When talking about
Korean actors who truly master English,
Oak Tan's name appears almost
automatically. Unlike many artists who
learn the language as adults due to
career demands, Tion built his fluency
naturally while still very young. He
spent a large part of his adolescence
living in the United States, which made
English practically a second language
for him, not just technically, but
emotionally and culturally.
>> Me being a detective trying to find a
serial killer, and it was very dark. It
was very
>> This is evident not only in his
impeccable pronunciation, but mainly in
the confidence with which he expresses
himself. [music] Techon doesn't think
before speaking English. He doesn't
translate mentally. He simply speaks. In
international interviews, American
shows, and global events, the actor
converses naturally, cracks jokes,
[music] answers complex questions, and
even improvises, something only those
with real fluency can do. Furthermore,
his international background directly
influenced his career. Taion has always
been seen as a global artist capable of
representing Korea abroad without
linguistic barriers. This explains why
he frequently takes on roles in
productions with international appeal
and communicates directly with foreign
fans without an interpreter. Tishan's
English isn't just correct. It is fluid,
spontaneous, and comfortable, placing
him on a completely different level from
actors who merely memorize lines. He is
without a doubt one of the most solid
examples of real bilingualism among
Korean actors. When Kim Minha gained
international recognition, many people
were surprised not only by her acting,
but by something equally impressive, her
extremely natural English. The actress
showed the world that it isn't just
about answering basic questions, but
about communicating with depth, emotion,
and clarity in another language.
>> Any idea [laughter] when it was um first
started?
>> Minha built her fluency through
intensive studies and international
preparation, especially while working on
global productions. Unlike rehearsed
English, what stands out about her is
the clear articulation, the correct
rhythm of the sentences, and the ability
to express complex feelings without
sounding mechanical. This is rare even
among actors who have studied abroad. In
interviews with foreign press, she
answers long questions, develops
complete ideas, and maintains natural
conversations with international
journalists. [music] There are no
excessive pauses nor visible insecurity.
Her English sounds confident, [music]
polite, and extremely professional,
exactly the standard required by major
international productions. Another
important point is that Kim Minha adapts
her tone according to the context. In
more formal interviews, she expresses
herself with precision and elegance. In
more relaxed moments, her English
becomes lighter and more spontaneous.
This shows not only technical knowledge
of the language, but real communicative
mastery. Park Shinhai is one of the
Korean actresses with the greatest
connection to the international
audience, especially in Latin America.
Over the years, this relationship became
even more evident when the actress
started communicating in Spanish,
surprising fans and foreign press. Even
though she is not fluent at a native
level, Park Shinhai demonstrates clear
understanding and security when
speaking, she takes risks, articulates
complete [music] sentences, and
maintains direct communication,
something many artists avoid for fear of
making mistakes. This shows not only
preparation, but respect for the
Spanish-speaking audience.
>> After hearing the actress herself speak,
it becomes evident that her Spanish is
not just rehearsed. There is
naturalenness, a spontaneous smile, and
a real effort to connect without
intermediaries. This detail has always
generated great repercussions among
fans, reinforcing her accessible and
approachable image. This skill helped
consolidate Park Shinhai as one of the
most beloved Korean actresses outside of
Korea, especially in Spanish-speaking
countries. Known for his sophisticated
and discreet image, Gong Yu also
surprised the public by demonstrating
familiarity with Spanish in
international appearances. Unlike long
conversational moments, his case stands
out precisely for the clarity and
confidence when expressing himself, even
in short phrases. When Gong Yu speaks
Spanish, the impact is immediate. The
pronunciation is careful, the rhythm is
natural, and the communicative intention
is clear. There is no exaggeration, nor
any attempt to appear to be something he
is not. Just [music] direct, elegant,
and efficient communication.
This kind of posture reinforces the
actor's international profile as he has
always been seen as someone prepared to
dialogue with different cultures. Even
without long speeches, he demonstrates
enough mastery to communicate and create
a real connection with a foreign
audience. It is yet another proof that
Gong Yu belongs to a select group of
Korean actors who manage to cross
linguistic barriers with naturalness.
When we talk about actors linked to the
Korean industry who actually speak
French, [music] Julian Kang is one of
the most solid and undeniable cases.
Unlike artists who learn the language
out of curiosity or specific necessity,
Julian grew up in a franophhone
environment. He was born in S Pierre and
Mikolon, a French territory, and is the
son of a French mother, so French is one
of his native languages. This life
experience makes all the difference.
Julian Kang's French doesn't sound
rehearsed or artificial. He masters the
rhythm, pronunciation, and natural
intonation of the language, something
only those who grow up hearing and
speaking French daily can develop. That
is why when he expresses himself in this
language, communication flows
spontaneously and comfortably. After
hearing Julian himself speak French, it
becomes evident that these are not
memorized phrases or short
introductions. He maintains complete
conversations, responds naturally, and
demonstrates absolute security in the
language. This detail places him in an
extremely select group within Korea
related entertainment. Julian Kang is
undoubtedly a real example of a
multilingual artist capable of moving
between cultures without linguistic
barriers, reinforcing his international
profile authentically. When we talk
about Korean artists with multiple
linguistic skills, Chaen Wu is
frequently cited as one of the names
that stand out beyond acting and music.
Although there isn't a clear and direct
video of him speaking French in an
interview or drama scene today, there
are reliable records and reports that he
has used or been observed communicating
in French at international events. Cha
Yun Wu has been present at major events
outside Korea, such as Paris Fashion
Week, where he not only interacted with
the international press, but also
greeted cameras and gave thanks in
French in front of the audience and
photographers, a gesture that goes
beyond a simple isolated word and
indicates a real familiarity with the
language. Furthermore, although most
public records show his fluent English
in full interviews, sources mention that
he manages to communicate in French in
media contexts and international events,
even if there aren't many clips
published specifically for that. This
skill stems from his frequent presence
in multicultural environments and his
constant exposure to foreign languages
as part of the global career he has
built. What stands out about Chaun Wu is
precisely this ability to switch between
various languages with naturalness, even
when there isn't a formal recording
easily accessible on video. Therefore,
we can include his skill as a valid
example of linguistic talent supported
by reliable sources and real
observations.
